summary: Jeff Atwood argues that given the high cost of programmer salaries versus the low cost of hardware, companies should default to solving performance problems by upgrading hardware rather than optimizing code. Using Stack Overflow as an example, he shows how $5,000 in servers delivered 50% better performance—less than one month of a programmer's salary. However, he acknowledges that hardware alone can't fix fundamentally bad algorithms, citing a classic example where a Trash-80 beat a DEC Alpha due to better code. He proposes a pragmatic approach: try hardware first, benchmark if needed, then optimize code only when necessary. The key insight is that premature optimization is dangerous and expensive, so companies should leverage the hardware-programmer cost imbalance strategically.
key_points: The cost of even modest programming teams vastly exceeds hardware costs, making hardware upgrades almost always the better first investment
Failing to provide programmers with good equipment is penny-wise and pound-foolish—a 5% productivity gain pays for itself within a year
No amount of fast hardware can compensate for poorly chosen algorithms or data structures
Code optimization is hard, dangerous, and tends to consume expensive developer time with diminishing returns
The recommended approach is iterative: throw hardware at the problem first, then benchmark and optimize only if still necessary
money_quotes: Hardware is cheap, and programmers are expensive. Whenever you're provided an opportunity to leverage that imbalance, it would be incredibly foolish not to.
The fastest hardware in the world can't save you from bad code.
Always try to spend your way out of a performance problem first by throwing faster hardware at it.
More computing sins are committed in the name of efficiency (without necessarily achieving it) than for any other single reason - including blind stupidity.
Optimizing your code is hard. And sometimes, dangerous. It is not something you undertake lightly.
If a one-time investment of $4,000 on each programmer makes them merely 5% more productive, you'll break even after the first year. Every year after that you've made a profit.
Everything is fast for small n. When n gets large, that's when things start to go sideways.
key_insight: Before investing expensive programmer time in optimization, leverage the cost disparity between cheap hardware and expensive developers—but recognize that no hardware can fix fundamentally flawed algorithms.
